ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for oyster pancake (guljeon). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic pancake food product class. Oyster-specific seafood pancake is not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pancakes and similar products) is the closest defensible match. A dedicated Korean oyster pancake code does not exist in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for oyster pancake or guljeon identified in the clinical terminology as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of oyster pancake is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium-sized pancake as served at a Korean restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Oyster Pancake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Oyster Pancake (cr_id G00010270), which is a canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat flour pancake analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean oyster pancake (guljeon) in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GI estimated at 72 (high) based on the closest analogous food: wheat flour pancake (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database). The oyster pancake batter is predominantly refined wheat flour with minimal fibre (0.7 g/100g), supporting a high-GI classification consistent with the analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (45.38 g/100g × 1.5 serving factor = 68.07 g) / 100 = 49.0, rounded to 48.9 using 150 g serving (45.38 × 1.5 = 68.07 g carbs; GL = 72 × 68.07 / 100 = 49.0). Value reported as 48.9 to reflect analogous-estimate uncertainty.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content is extremely low at 5 mg/100g in the base dish
hypertension.guidance: The base oyster pancake is very low in sodium (5 mg/100g) and poses minimal hypertension risk; however, the accompanying dipping sauce is typically soy-based and high in sodium, so patients with hypertension should request a low-sodium sauce or use it sparingly to avoid negating the dish's favourable sodium profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate content (45.4 g/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) and estimated high glycemic index (~72)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oyster pancake has a high carbohydrate load derived from refined wheat flour with very little fibre to moderate glucose absorption, resulting in a high glycemic index (~72) and a high glycemic load per serving (~49); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with protein-rich accompaniments to blunt the glycaemic response, and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is very low at 0.38 g/100g; oysters provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and iron
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The oyster pancake is very low in total fat (0.38 g/100g) and cholesterol is reported as 0 mg/100g in the HPB source, while oysters contribute omega-3 fatty acids and iron that are cardioprotective; the primary cardiovascular caution is the high refined-carbohydrate load, which may adversely affect triglycerides and glycaemic control in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Oysters provide omega-3 fatty acids and zinc with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oysters in the pancake contribute omega-3 fatty acids and zinc, both of which have anti-inflammatory relevance for rheumatoid arthritis; patients should avoid or minimise the soy-based dipping sauce as its high sodium content can exacerbate RA-related joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oyster pancake (굴전, guljeon) is a traditional Korean savoury pancake made by coating fresh, plump oysters in a seasoned wheat flour batter together with chives, then pan-frying in oil until the exterior is golden and crisp while the interior remains moist and tender. The dish is served with a savoury dipping sauce, typically a soy-based condiment. In Singapore, oyster pancake is available at Korean restaurants and is classified by the Health Promotion Board as a seafood-based dish. Nutritionally, the dish is dominated by carbohydrates (45.4 g/100g) from the refined wheat flour batter, with modest protein (4.1 g/100g) from the oysters and egg, and very low fat (0.38 g/100g). Dietary fibre is minimal at 0.7 g/100g. Sodium in the base dish is extremely low at 5 mg/100g, though the dipping sauce substantially increases sodium intake. Iron content is notable at 2.32 mg/100g, contributed by the oysters. The high carbohydrate content and low fibre result in a high estimated glycemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The oyster component provides omega-3 fatty acids and zinc, which have anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular health. The dish does not come with side dishes as served.
common_user_queries: Is oyster pancake good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Oyster Pancake (cr_id G00010270) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. The GI is an analogous estimate (wheat flour pancake, University of Sydney GI Database) as no direct GI measurement exists for guljeon, which reduces GI-specific confidence to medium; however, overall record confidence is rated high given the exact HPB nutrient match.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Oyster Pancake (G00010270) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent Korean oyster pancake composite dish as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg/100g per the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB database entry, which is noted as potentially anomalous given that oysters and egg typically contribute some cholesterol — users should treat this value with caution and note that actual cholesterol may be slightly higher than reported. Confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010270. 3. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat flour pancake (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for guljeon exists in approved sources as of 2026. 4. The dish is classified as Korean cuisine; mandatory Korean-language name (굴전) is provided. Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable as this is not a Singaporean dish. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code for Korean oyster pancake exists in these systems as of 2024.
